tree.heading: Overview
tree.content_preview: This guideline covers care and treatment for people with, or at risk of, chronic kidney disease (CKD). It aims to prevent or delay the progression, and reduce the risk of complications and cardiovascu

tree.heading: Who is it for?
tree.content_preview:
Healthcare professionals
Commissioners and providers
People with CKD, their families and carers

tree.heading: Recommendations
tree.content_preview:
People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in NICE's information on making decisions about your care.

Making decisions using NICE g

tree.heading: Incidental finding of proteinuria on reagent strips
tree.content_preview: 1.1.15 If unexplained proteinuria is an incidental finding on a reagent strip, offer testing for CKD using eGFRcreatinine and ACR. [2021]

tree.content_preview:
1.1.20 Monitor GFR at least annually in adults, children and young people who are taking

medicines that can adversely affect kidney function, such as calcineurin inhibitors (for example, ciclosporin

children.heading: 1.2 Classification of CKD in adults
children.content_preview:
1.2.1 Classify CKD in adults using a combination of GFR and ACR categories (as described in table 1). Be aware that:
increased ACR is associated with increased risk of adverse outcomes
decreased GFR i

tree.heading: Investigating the cause of CKD and determining the risk of adverse outcomes
tree.content_preview: 1.2.3 Agree a plan to establish the cause of CKD during an informed discussion with the person with CKD, particularly if the cause may be treatable (for example, urinary tract obstruction, medicines t

tree.heading: Indications for renal ultrasound in adults
tree.content_preview:
1.2.5 Offer a renal ultrasound scan to all adults with CKD who:
have accelerated progression of CKD (see recommendation 1.3.5)
have visible or persistent invisible haematuria
have symptoms of urinary

children.heading: 1.3 Frequency of monitoring
children.content_preview: 1.3.1 If an adult, child or young person has CKD, or is at risk of it, agree the frequency of monitoring (eGFRcreatinine and ACR) with them (and their family members or carers, as appropriate), bearin
ancestor_path: Indications for renal ultrasound in adults

tree.heading: Defining progression in adults
tree.content_preview:
1.3.5 Define accelerated progression of CKD in adults as:
a sustained decrease in GFR of 25% or more and a change in GFR category

tree.heading: within 12 months or
tree.content_preview:
a sustained decrease in GFR of 15 ml/min/1.73 m 2 per year. [2014]
1.3.6 Take the following steps to identify the rate of progression of CKD:
Obtain a minimum of 3 GFR estimations over a period of not

tree.heading: Risk factors associated with CKD progression in adults
tree.content_preview:
1.3.9 Work with adults who have any of the following risk factors for CKD progression to optimise their health:
cardiovascular disease
proteinuria
previous episode of acute kidney injury
hypertension

children.heading: 1.4 Information and education for people with CKD
children.content_preview: 1.4.1 Offer people with CKD (and their family members or carers, as appropriate) education and information tailored to the severity and cause of CKD, the associated complications and the risk of progr
ancestor_path: Risk factors associated with CKD progression in adults

tree.heading: Lifestyle advice
tree.content_preview: 1.4.6 Encourage adults with CKD to take exercise, achieve a healthy weight and stop smoking. [2008]

tree.heading: Dietary interventions
tree.content_preview:
1.4.7 Offer dietary advice about potassium, phosphate, calorie and salt intake appropriate to the severity of CKD. [2008, amended 2014]
1.4.8 If dietary intervention is agreed, provide it alongside ed

tree.heading: Low-protein diets
tree.content_preview: 1.4.9 Do not offer low-protein diets (dietary protein intake less than 0.6 to 0.8 g/kg/ day) to adults with CKD. [2014]

tree.heading: Self-management
tree.content_preview:

inform adults with CKD (and their family members or carers, as appropriate) of their diagnosis
enable adults with CKD (and their family members or carers, a

a 5-year risk of needing renal replacement therapy of grea

－ the recommendations on patient views and preferences in NICE's guideline on pa

tree.content_preview: 1.6.24 Follow the recommendations in NICE's guideline on cardiovascular disease: risk assessment and reduction, including lipid modification for the use of statins in adults with CKD. [2014]

tree.content_preview:

If eGFR is above 60 ml/min/1.73 m 2 , investigate other causes of anaemia as it is unlikely to be caused by CKD.

tree.heading: Starting erythropoietic stimulating agent therapy in iron deficiency
tree.content_preview: 1.8.1 ESA (erythropoietic stimulating agent) therapy should not be started in the presence of absolute iron deficiency without also managing the iron deficiency. [2006]

tree.heading: Nutritional supplements
tree.content_preview: 1.8.10 Do not prescribe supplements of vitamin C, folic acid or carnitine as adjuvants specifically for the treatment of anaemia of CKD. [2006]

tree.heading: Androgens
tree.content_preview: 1.8.11 Do not use androgens to treat anaemia in people with anaemia of CKD. [2006]

tree.heading: Secondary hyperparathyroidism
tree.content_preview:
1.8.12 Treat clinically relevant secondary hyperparathyroidism in adults, children and young people with CKD to improve the management of the anaemia. [2006]

tree.heading: Person-centred care and ESAs
tree.content_preview: 1.8.15 Give adults, children and young people offered ESA therapy and their GPs information about why ESA therapy is needed, how it works and what benefits and side effects may be experienced. [2006]

tree.heading: Patient education programmes
tree.content_preview: 1.8.20 Offer culturally and age-appropriate patient education programmes to all adults, children and young people diagnosed with anaemia of CKD (and their families and carers). These should be repeate

tree.heading: Benefits of treatment with ESAs
tree.content_preview: 1.9.1 Offer treatment with ESAs to adults, children and young people with anaemia of CKD who are likely to benefit in terms of quality of life and physical function. [2006]

tree.heading: Blood transfusions
tree.content_preview:
1.9.2 Avoid blood transfusions if possible in people with anaemia of CKD in whom kidney transplant is a treatment option. [2006]
1.9.3 If a transfusion is indicated clinically in a person with anaemia

the person's dialysis status
the route of administration
the

their preferences

the necessary treatment. [2011]

the aspirational range is not achieved despite escalating ESA doses.

children.heading: 1.9.13 Do not use age alone to determine treatment of anaemia of CKD. [2006]

tree.heading: Terms used in this guideline
tree.content_preview: This section defines terms that have been used in a particular way for this guideline. For other definitions see the NICE glossary.

tree.heading: Chronic kidney disease (CKD)
tree.content_preview: Abnormalities of kidney function or structure present for more than 3 months, with implications for health. This includes all people with markers of kidney damage and those with a glomerular filtratio

tree.heading: Classification of CKD
tree.content_preview:
CKD is classified according to estimated GFR (eGFR) and albumin:creatinine ratio (ACR)

(see table 1), using 'G' to denote the GFR category (G1 to G5, which have the same GFR thresholds as the CKD sta

tree.heading: Glomerular filtration rate (GFR)
tree.content_preview:
This is abbreviated in the following way in this guideline:
GFR: either a measured or an estimated GFR
eGFR: estimated GFR (without indicating the method of estimation)
eGFRcreatinine: an estimation o

tree.heading: 4-variable Kidney Failure Risk Equation
tree.content_preview:
A person's 5-year risk of needing renal replacement therapy (defined as the need for dialysis or transplant) is estimated, as in Major 2019, as:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded

tree.heading: Markers of kidney damage
tree.content_preview: These include albuminuria (ACR more than 3 mg/mmol), urine sediment abnormalities, electrolyte and other abnormalities due to tubular disorders, abnormalities detected by histology, structural abnorma

tree.heading: Pre-dialysis
tree.content_preview: Usually regarded to be CKD stages 4 and 5, although there is no accepted definition. Predialysis includes people with a failing transplant and people having conservative management.

tree.heading: Renal replacement therapy (RRT)
tree.content_preview: Life-supporting treatments for severe acute kidney injury or stage 5 CKD. This includes haemodialysis, haemofiltration, haemodiafiltration, peritoneal dialysis and kidney transplantation.

tree.heading: Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system antagonist
tree.content_preview: A medicine that blocks or inhibits the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, including angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors, angiotensin-receptor blockers (ARBs), direct renin inhibitors and

tree.heading: Renin-angiotensin system antagonist
tree.content_preview: A medicine that blocks or inhibits the renin-angiotensin system, including ACE inhibitors, ARBs and direct renin inhibitors. This group of medicines does not include aldosterone antagonists.

tree.heading: Rationale and impact
tree.content_preview: These sections briefly explain why the committee made the recommendations and how they might affect practice.

tree.heading: Creatinine-based estimate of glomerular filtration rate (GFR)
Why the committee did not make recommendations
tree.content_preview: Evidence on the specific eGFR equations or ethnicity adjustments seen by the committee was not from UK studies so may not be applicable to UK black, Asian and minority ethnic groups. None of the studi

tree.heading: Impact on practice
tree.content_preview: There will be an impact on practice, as the adjustment of the CKD-EPI creatinine equation for adults of African-Caribbean or African family origin has been removed from the guideline. Only a small num

tree.heading: Investigations for proteinuria
tree.content_preview: Recommendations 1.1.10 to 1.1.14

tree.heading: Why the committee made the recommendations
tree.content_preview: For children and young people with CKD, there was no evidence for the accuracy of measuring albumin:creatinine ratio (ACR) compared with protein:creatinine ratio (PCR) to quantify proteinuria. The com

tree.heading: How the recommendations might affect practice
tree.content_preview:
The recommendations are in line with current practice, so no additional resources should be needed.

tree.heading: How the recommendations might affect practice

tree.heading: Pharmacotherapy for blood pressure control
tree.content_preview: Recommendations 1.6.1 to 1.6.3

tree.heading: Why the committee made the recommendations
tree.content_preview: Results from a meta-analysis (including the SPRINT trial) showed no meaningful difference between standard and more intensive blood pressure targets for adults with CKD. The 2014 guideline recommended

tree.heading: How the recommendations might affect practice
tree.content_preview: The recommendations for adults are consistent with current practice and should not have an impact on resources. The recommendation for blood pressure targets in children and young people may have some

tree.heading: Pharmacotherapy for proteinuria and choice of antihypertensive agent
tree.content_preview: Recommendations 1.6.5 to 1.6.12

tree.heading: Why the committee made the recommendations
tree.content_preview: The interventions recommended are intended to improve a range of outcomes, including rates of progression to end-stage renal disease. There was evidence for adults, but not for children and young peop

tree.heading: People without diabetes
tree.content_preview: The evidence showed that, compared with placebo, ACE inhibitors reduced the risk of end-stage renal disease in people without diabetes. ARBs did not show the same effect. However, the committee did no

tree.heading: People with type 2 diabetes
tree.content_preview: For people with type 2 diabetes, ARBs reduced the risk of end-stage renal disease and heart failure. The committee also recommended ACE inhibitors because the evidence did not show a clear difference

tree.heading: How the recommendations might affect practice
tree.content_preview:
The recommendations reflect current practice, so no additional resources should be needed.

Return to recommendations

tree.heading: Which phosphate binders to use for children and young people
tree.content_preview: The committee reviewed the recommendations from the 2013 guideline in the light of limited new evidence. For children and young people with high serum calcium, they agreed to recommend sevelamer carbo

tree.heading: Which phosphate binders to use for adults
tree.content_preview: The committee reviewed the evidence for phosphate binders both in adults on dialysis and adults not having dialysis. Although the evidence for those not on dialysis was limited, it did reflect the evi

tree.heading: carbonate
tree.content_preview:
lanthanum carbonate is much more expensive than calcium acetate and sevelamer carbonate and may provide less benefit than other non-calcium-based phosphate binders.

Based on this evidence, the commit

tree.heading: How the recommendations might affect practice
tree.content_preview:
Replacing sevelamer hydrochloride with sevelamer carbonate may result in lower resource use, because there is a cheap generic version of sevelamer carbonate available.

tree.heading: Context
tree.content_preview: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) describes abnormal kidney function or structure. It is common and often occurs with other conditions (such as cardiovascular disease and diabetes). Moderate to severe CKD
